video_title: Why Have Bitcoin and Gold Prices Crashed? Is $40,000 BTC's Approaching Bottom?
gist: Ledn co-founder Mauricio Di Bartolomeo announces the platform is adding tokenized gold (Tether Gold/XAUT), full USDT support, USAT and gold-backed loans, and argues Bitcoin's drop to ~$60k shows seller exhaustion rather than a path to the $40k many expect. He details Ledn's investment-grade ABS bond as the funding rail for a Bitcoin-backed loan market he sees reaching $1 trillion in 5-10 years, and gives an on-the-ground read on post-Maduro Venezuela. Despite the title framing a 'crash,' the episode is largely a product/credit-strategy interview.

insights.claim: Ledn is listing Tether Gold (XAUT) so clients can buy and hold tokenized gold alongside Bitcoin, with gold-backed loans to follow later in the year.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Signals crypto lenders diversifying collateral beyond Bitcoin into tokenized gold; watch for gold-collateralized credit as a new product category.
insights.quote: we're going to be listing XUT. So gold is coming to the Letin platform.

insights.claim: Ledn is rolling out full USDT support across all products and listing USAT, Tether's US Genius Act-compliant stablecoin, positioning stablecoins as core rails for disbursements and repayments.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: US-regulated stablecoin adoption by lenders is a leading indicator of the Genius Act reshaping crypto credit distribution.

insights.claim: Tokenizing gold via a custodied receipt (XAUT) does not add counterparty risk versus the status quo because most gold holders already vault their gold with a third party, but it adds transferability, divisibility and collateral utility.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'paper gold receipt' objection; supports a thesis that tokenized gold demand grows as physical holders seek liquidity and financing.
insights.quote: it's a very akin to hoarding a big pile of cash in your house versus having some dollar stable coins

insights.claim: Tether custodies its gold reserves in Swiss vaults and reportedly now owns more gold than Bitcoin, with each XAUT token representing a specific bar held by Tether.
insights.speaker: host and guest
insights.so_what: Tether's shift into physical gold and land makes it a notable non-sovereign gold buyer worth tracking for commodity flows.
insights.quote: Tether now actually I think owns more gold than Bitcoin. They've been buying gold. They've been buying land.

insights.claim: Bitcoin retested ~$60k in this drawdown just as it did in February, but unlike February there were few forced liquidations and buyers stepped in, which the guest reads as seller exhaustion rather than the start of a deeper leg down.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Loan-book behavior at $60k is a real-money sentiment gauge suggesting the retest was absorbed, not capitulation.
insights.quote: I have not seen any single person at this conference being like, "Yeah, I'm selling at 60. I'm done."

insights.claim: The guest argues the widely expected drop into the $40,000s is unlikely precisely because the move is so telegraphed, drawing a parallel to the failed consensus that Bitcoin would run to $150k.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: A contrarian fade of the consensus $40k-bottom call; positions for a shallower dip and fall rally.
insights.quote: makes me think that it is rather unlikely that we're going to go test the 40s

insights.claim: Bitcoin held steady while the S&P 500 fell 5-6% over three days and gold and silver were hammered the prior week, an intermarket divergence the guest reads as Bitcoin bottoming.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Relative strength versus equities and metals during a selloff is a tell to watch for a Bitcoin trend change.
insights.quote: We've been seeing the S&P now down five, six% over the last three days. And Bitcoin's holding steady.

insights.claim: The guest expects Bitcoin to stay under pressure through summer then rally into the US midterms, resolving to the upside in the fall, with the SpaceX IPO potentially minting ~4,000 new millionaires as fresh demand.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: A calendar/liquidity roadmap (summer weakness, fall rally, IPO wealth effect) for timing Bitcoin exposure.
insights.quote: I think this resolves to the upside sometime in the fall.

insights.claim: Counterintuitively, borrowing demand at Ledn rose as Bitcoin fell because clients who were uncomfortable borrowing at the $125k highs now borrow at lower prices to buy the dip or reduce downside risk.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Loan demand is counter-cyclical to price, meaning Bitcoin-lending revenue can be resilient in drawdowns.
insights.quote: people are only borrowing when the market's high but the opposite is also true

insights.claim: Ledn originates loans at 50% loan-to-value as a deliberate design choice to give a large cushion, and the guest argues 70% LTV products from competitors are unsafe because they leave too little room to respond to volatility.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: LTV policy differentiates lenders on liquidation risk; a screen for which platforms survive drawdowns.
insights.quote: There are companies out there that will say, "Oh, yeah, borrow up to 70. It's all good."

insights.claim: No Ledn client who enabled auto top-up with a sufficient balance has ever been liquidated, and the guest urges borrowers to budget 50-100% of their initial collateral for margin calls.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Actionable risk protocol for anyone using Bitcoin-backed loans; liquidations are largely a behavior/planning failure.
insights.quote: No single client that has turned on auto topup with a sufficient balance has gotten liquidated.

insights.claim: Ledn liquidations are fully automated with no human intervention because discretionary leniency on one underwater loan would put the entire loan book at risk.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Explains why borrowers get no grace period; underscores collateral-management discipline as non-negotiable.
insights.quote: there is no human intervention on this matter

insights.claim: The guest frames the anti-credit faction in Bitcoin as a values dispute rather than a real fault line, noting many OGs and influential Bitcoiners quietly use Ledn to borrow rather than sell and defer taxes.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Suggests the addressable market for Bitcoin credit is larger than the vocal self-custody purists imply.
insights.quote: there might be a little bit of posturing I would say in terms of like you have to tow a certain line

insights.claim: The guest characterizes a Bitcoin-backed loan as economically equivalent to a mortgage, framing both as a short on the dollar collateralized by a hard asset.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for positioning Bitcoin credit to mainstream, mortgage-comfortable investors.
insights.quote: A mortgage is a short on the dollar backed by a property.

themes.theme: Ledn institutional bond and market sizing

insights.claim: Ledn forecasts the consumer Bitcoin-backed loan market growing from roughly $3 billion today to $1 trillion within 5-10 years, driven by rising collateral-adoption rates on a rising Bitcoin market cap.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: A large TAM thesis for Bitcoin credit; the funding-market buildout is the enabling constraint to watch.
insights.quote: we think that the Bitcoin back loan market can reach a trillion dollars within 5 to 10 years

insights.claim: Only about 2% of Bitcoin is currently used as loan collateral, matching the ~2% of equities used for margin but far below the 60-70% of homes carrying a mortgage, implying large room for collateral-adoption growth.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Quantifies the penetration runway for Bitcoin as collateral versus other asset classes.
insights.quote: only 2% of Bitcoin is being used as collateral for consumer loans

insights.claim: Ledn says it holds roughly 30% of the global consumer Bitcoin-backed loan market, meaning one in three such loans worldwide are originated on its platform.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Establishes Ledn as the dominant incumbent, a benchmark for competitors and potential acquirers.
insights.quote: let holds 30% of that market. So, one in three loans globally are done at Latin.

insights.claim: Ledn issued what it calls the first investment-grade rated ABS bond for Bitcoin-backed loans, working with S&P for ~1.5 years, using Fidelity as custodian and Jefferies as underwriter, on a ~$1 billion loan book with eight years and zero defaults.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Investment-grade securitization opens pension/endowment funding for Bitcoin credit; a template rivals must match.
insights.quote: we did get investment grade. The bond offering was two times over subscribe on the senior, three times on the junior.

insights.claim: The guest distinguishes a Ledn bond from a MicroStrategy STRC preferred: the Ledn bond is a bankruptcy-remote SPV claim on individual dollar loans collateralized by Bitcoin at Fidelity with a fixed clipped yield, whereas STRC's performance depends on the issuer's treasury management.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Clarifies risk differences between superficially similar Bitcoin credit instruments for yield investors.
insights.quote: It's a bankruptcy remote SPV structure

insights.claim: Ledn issued the ABS bond because no single balance sheet can fund a trillion-dollar loan book, so it copied the mortgage and auto-loan playbook of packaging loans and selling them into the institutional securitization market that requires investment-grade ratings.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Explains why securitization, not deposits, is the scaling mechanism for Bitcoin credit and why ratings gate access to big buyers.
insights.quote: 90 to 95% of all issuances in this bond market have to be what's called investment grade

insights.claim: The guest reports Maduro was removed via a US military operation (dated 'Jan 3') with essentially no armed resistance from the regime or population, marking a potential inflection for Venezuela though far from a resolved transition.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: A regime change in a major oil/gold nation reopens sovereign-risk and reconstruction investment questions.
insights.quote: there was not a single shot fired back at the Apaches

insights.claim: Since the regime change, Venezuelan oil production has at least doubled and reportedly more than tripled, and the government is moving to clean up illegal gold mining, which the guest attributes to anticipated entry by US rare-earth and mining companies.
insights.speaker: Mauricio Di Bartolomeo, co-founder Ledn
insights.so_what: Rising Venezuelan oil output and reopened mining could shift commodity supply and create early-mover resource plays.
insights.quote: oil production's more than tripled or or doubled at least since Jan 3rd
